replies.author: @ob1coyote
replies.text:
@AmericaFirst1973 Since you asked...
Teachers’ unions are among the most politically active labor organizations in the United States. Because they overwhelmingly support progressive candidates and policies, critics argue that they create structural pathways for left‑of‑center activism to influence public education through political funding and endorsements, collective bargaining agreements that include language that shapes school culture,  Professional development requirements that include equity, anti‑bias, or culturally responsive training. Contractual protections that make it difficult to remove teachers who incorporate political content into lessons. Limits on classroom observation or evaluation, reducing oversight of instructional content. 
Unions often sponsor or co‑sponsor workshops, conferences, and training sessions that frequently include frameworks rooted in progressive social theory (e.g., anti‑racism, gender‑inclusive pedagogy). Teachers who attend often bring those frameworks into their classrooms, and - Union‑aligned training can become the de facto professional culture in a district.
Unions don’t write curriculum, but they lobby for or against certain educational standards supporting ethnic studies requirements, opposing legislation that restricts discussion of race, gender identity, or sexuality, and advocating for social‑emotional learning frameworks that critics view as ideological.
Unions often provide legal defense for teachers who feel porotected to introduce controversial or politically charged material, make administrators hesitate ro discipline teachers for fear of union pushback and basically create a permissive environment for activism in the classroom.

Internal newsletters, email blasts, and conferences often promote political causes making teachers feel social pressure to align with union‑endorsed positions. Political messaging can become normalized within the professional environment. Union‑organized walkouts or protests (e.g., “Red for Ed”) can blend labor issues with broader political themes.

Teachers’ unions frequently partner with civil rights groups, community organizers, and progressive nonprofits.

Summing that all up as "protecting teachers' rights" is seriously disingenuous, like calling abortion "healthcare."
Regardless of your political opinions, you don't take EVERYONE's tax dollars to indoctrinate students with one political view, and if you can't see that happening, or won't see it happening because your union get's you a raise from time to time, then yes, you are the problem.

replies.author: @Amberlyte75
replies.text: Wrong.  We NEVER had a clue about our teacher's political views in the 1960s, 1970s, 1980s.  We knew if the female teachers were married if their names were presented as 'Mrs'.  Now these kids don't even know what their teachers ARE other than ugly puppet masters

replies.author: @the1magageneral323
replies.text: @Amberlyte75 Really? You expect me to believe that even during the 1960s that teachers did not tell students to protest for civil rights movement or against the Vietnam war. Those "student protests" even then seem like teachers and someone else were just telling them what to think, instead of how to think critically. Remember one kind of media and even with the tv fake news existed back then too and the press lied to mislead the masses.

replies.author: @Amberlyte75
replies.text: ​@the1magageneral323 i hope you believe, it's the truth.  While COLLEGES and UNIVERSITIES had open protests, there were NONE at high schools.  In fact, the very first Earth day in 1971 had high school students walk out of their classes and gather in a common area of the school.  Who were then sent back to the classrooms under very real threat of expulsion.  Nobody stopped traffic.  Nobody broke things.   Nobody called for the death of law enforcement.  We were still in Viet Nam, our relatives, boyfriends, graduated classmates were being killed in a country most of us had never heard of before to try and FREE one half of Viet Nam from a truly oppressive regime.  A scant few years later we took in many, many thousands of Vietnamese refugees,   WHO were ASSIMILATED and became a part of our society, didn't LIVE off of the taxpayers.  Any teacher who would have dared push their lifestyle, idealogy, religion, politics,  or hatred onto even ONE student would have been FIRED IMMEDIATELY and  probably beaten up by at least one student's father.  We learned to disagree without feeling we needed to crush anyone who believed what we did not.   Grade school kids can't even decide most things in their lives, but they know that they hate Trump, they hate ICE ?   Sorry .... we lived in better times

author: @samrester6254
text:
Federal money to schools is based on attendance.  A student has to spend X-hours at school during that day, for that district to qualify for federal money, per student, per day.

That is why it was set after 2p.m.  If this was just a normal rally, it would have started earlier, like the others.    This is how you know where it is stemming from.  These 'educators' know that if they don't mess with the district's federal hand out, they will not come under that much scrutiny.
